ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists for 'Beijing Duck Seafood Dish'. Mapped to the nearest parent class 'seafood dish' (FOODON_03315595) as a closest_parent match. The dish is a Chinese-style stir-fried or braised crab and lobster preparation sold under the menu name 'Beijing Duck' at Riverfront Seafood, Singapore.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — closest FoodEx2 category for a mixed crab and lobster cooked dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for a composite Chinese-style crab-and-lobster restaurant dish. Closest_parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite seafood restaurant dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular codes for mixed crustacean preparations of this type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of this Chinese-style crab and lobster dish is approximately 500 g, typically shared between two to three diners as part of a multi-dish Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Crustaceans, crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175180) and Crustaceans, lobster, northern, cooked, moist heat (FDC ID 175185), combined with Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Food for sauce-adjusted composite
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite restaurant dish sold under the menu name 'Beijing Duck' at Riverfront Seafood, Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked crab (FDC ID 175180) and cooked lobster (FDC ID 175185), blended in approximate equal proportion, with sauce-contribution adjustments (soy sauce, oyster sauce) referenced against Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Food. Input values (protein 11 g, fat 8 g, carbohydrate 4 g, energy 130 kcal, sodium 360 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where the composite adaptation does not yield a defensible per-100 g figure.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on cooked shellfish/crustacean category (GI ~15, low-carbohydrate protein-dominant food)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Chinese-style crab and lobster restaurant dish. GI is estimated at approximately 15 based on the analogous category of cooked crustaceans (shellfish), which are protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate foods with a well-established low GI profile per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Carbohydrate content is 4.0 g per 100 g; GL per 500 g serving = (15 × 20 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.0. This is a low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg per 100 g from soy sauce and oyster sauce; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request sauce served on the side or ask for reduced-sauce preparation to limit sodium intake; a 500 g serving of this dish as prepared contributes approximately 1800 mg sodium and is not suitable for unrestricted consumption in a hypertension management diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content is low at 4.0 g per 100 g (20 g per 500 g serving) with sugar only 1.0 g per 100 g; high protein from crab and lobster (11.0 g per 100 g) slows gastric emptying and moderates postprandial glucose response; estimated GI is approximately 15 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without additional starchy sides; request reduced oyster sauce to minimise added sugars and cornstarch thickening to avoid refined carbohydrate loading.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol is 60 mg per 100 g (300 mg per 500 g serving), approaching the traditional 300 mg daily dietary cholesterol threshold; saturated fat is moderate at 2.0 g per 100 g; sodium at 360 mg per 100 g is a significant cardiovascular risk factor; however, crab and lobster provide omega-3 fatty acids with anti-atherogenic properties.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200–250 g, request reduced-sauce preparation to lower sodium, and account for the 300 mg cholesterol contribution of a full 500 g serving within their daily dietary cholesterol budget; the omega-3 content of crab and lobster is a mitigating factor.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Crab and lobster provide omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein supporting anti-inflammatory pathways; saturated fat is low at 2.0 g per 100 g; however, sodium at 360 mg per 100 g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its omega-3 content; request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce to limit sodium-driven inflammation, and monitor personal response to chilli as a nightshade ingredient.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijing Duck Seafood Dish is a Chinese-style restaurant dish sold at Riverfront Seafood in Singapore under the menu name 'Beijing Duck', despite containing no duck. The dish is a composite preparation of crab and lobster (crustaceans), stir-fried or braised in a sauce of soy sauce, oyster sauce, ginger, garlic, and chilli — a cooking style common to Singaporean Chinese seafood restaurants. The name is a menu anomaly; the food group, ingredients, and nutritional profile are entirely consistent with a Chinese-style crustacean seafood dish. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 130 kcal, 11 g protein, 8 g fat (2 g saturated), 4 g carbohydrate, 1 g sugar, 0.4 g dietary fibre, and 360 mg sodium. Cholesterol is 60 mg per 100 g, reflecting the naturally higher cholesterol content of shellfish. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 15 and a low glycemic load per 500 g serving of 3.0, making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium is the primary clinical concern, with a 500 g serving delivering approximately 1800 mg sodium from sauce ingredients. The dish is not suitable for unrestricted consumption by individuals managing hypertension. It is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management when consumed without starchy accompaniments. Crab and lobster contribute omega-3 fatty acids relevant to cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not halal-certified. Allergens include crustaceans, shellfish, soy, and gluten from oyster sauce.

confidence_reason: The dish is a composite restaurant preparation sold under a misleading menu name ('Beijing Duck') at Riverfront Seafood, Singapore. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked crab and cooked lobster with sauce adjustments referenced against Singapore HPB data. Input values from the GrabFood listing are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Confidence is medium because the nutrient profile is a defensible adaptation rather than an exact approved-source match.
notes: 1. DISH IDENTITY ANOMALY: This record is listed under the menu name 'Beijing Duck' but contains no duck. Based on the food group (Fish and seafood), food subgroup (Crab and Lobster dishes), and cuisine tags (Seafood Dish, Crab, Lobster) from the source GrabFood listing at Riverfront Seafood, Singapore, this is a Chinese-style crab and lobster preparation. The canonical food name has been corrected accordingly. 2. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked crab (FDC ID 175180) and cooked lobster (FDC ID 175185) with sauce adjustments. Input values are consistent with this adaptation and retained with medium confidence. 3. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, mineral micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite sauce-adjusted restaurant preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Nutrient Composition of Food database as of 2024. 4. GI ESTIMATION: GI of approximately 15 is an analogous estimate based on the cooked crustacean category per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). No direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 5. HALAL STATUS: Halal certification status of this dish at Riverfront Seafood is not confirmed; is_halal is set to false pending verification. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for this composite Chinese-style crab and lobster restaurant dish sold under a misleading menu name.
